title: Chapter 30: Inside the Glass Tower
content: The audit clause in Section 14.3 allows for physical inspection of the company's data facilities. Evelyn filed the request at 8:00 AM. By noon, I have an appointment at Mephisto MCN's registered office: 1 World Trade Center, Floor 67.
I'm standing at the base of the tallest building in the Western Hemisphere, looking up at 1,776 feet of glass and steel and American hubris, and I'm about to walk into Hell's branch office.

The irony is not lost on me. I can't feel irony anymore — can't feel the sting of it, the hot pinch of recognition — but I can recognize it the way I recognize a vulnerability in a codebase. It's there. It's structural. It's load-bearing.

One World Trade Center rises from the footprint of destruction into a monument of capitalism so pure it borders on worship. The glass facade catches the June sun and throws it back like a dare. The Spire tops out at the symbolic height — 1,776 feet, the number that means freedom, inscribed in the one material that has never meant freedom at all.

I cross the plaza. My heels click on polished stone. The audit clause doesn't specify I have to wear a suit, but Evelyn's voice was very clear on the phone: "Look like you belong. Look like you're someone they can't turn away." So I'm in my best black blazer, my only good pumps, and the hollow space behind my ribs where my trust used to live.

Security checks my ID. Security checks the audit authorization. Security checks my bag. Security looks at me — really looks — and I watch the flicker of something pass across her face. Not suspicion. Recognition. She sees the vacancy in me the same way I'm about to see it in everyone else in this building.

The elevator to Floor 67 is express. It moves so fast my stomach drops. The taste of the recycled air hits me — sterile, metallic, with a faint chemical sweetness, like someone tried to flavor the climate control and gave up halfway. I swallow it down.
Floor 67. The doors open onto a lobby that shouldn't exist.

It's all glass and white marble and impossible geometry. The walls curve where they should be flat. The ceiling vaults where it should be level. The light comes from everywhere and nowhere, reflecting off surfaces that don't quite align, creating the optical illusion of a space bigger on the inside than the outside. It's M.C. Escher if Escher had a design budget and a complete disregard for physics.
The receptionist is beautiful. Professional. And she has the same hollow look I've seen in the mirror every morning for three months.
She's a Creator. She's been harvested.

I know it before I check my phone. The slight delay in her smile. The microsecond pause before she makes eye contact, like the signal has to travel further than it should. The way her warmth reads as performative, not because she's faking it but because the part of her that made it genuine has been clipped and catalogued.
"Chloe Hart for the 12 PM audit inspection," I say.
"Welcome to Mephisto MCN." Her smile is perfect. White teeth, pink lips, not a single microexpression out of place. "I'm Jessica. Let me notify the compliance team."
While she types, I pull up the Hollowed Files on my phone. I search her name.
Jessica Tran. 1.2 million followers. Status: ACTIVE. Current deduction: trust (processing).

She's being drained right now. As she smiles at me from behind the desk, as her fingers move across the keyboard, as her voice says "Someone will be right with you," her trust is being siphoned out of her in increments too small to notice unless you're looking at the spec sheet.
Which I am.
"Thank you, Jessica," I say, and I mean it, and the meaning is the only sincerity left in this room.
The compliance officer comes out three minutes later. He's tall, professional, mid-thirties, and hollow.
Same look. Same delay. Same pause before warmth.

I don't check the Files for him. I don't need to. This entire floor is staffed by the harvested. Mephisto doesn't need human employees when he can use the people he's already taken. They're efficient. They're loyal. They have no emotional bandwidth left to question the architecture of their own exploitation.
"Ms. Hart, I'm David Chen, Senior Compliance Officer. I'll be conducting your facility tour today."
"Great. I'd like to see the server room first."
"Of course. Right this way."

He leads me through a corridor of glass and light. Every wall is transparent. Every office is visible. It's a design meant to convey openness. What it actually conveys is surveillance. Everyone can see everyone. No one can hide.

The server room is behind a frosted glass door that David opens with a keycard and a biometric scan. The cold hits me first — a wall of refrigerated air that settles on my skin like a second layer, prickling the hair on my forearms, icing the sweat that formed in the warm corridor. The room is dark. The only light comes from the status LEDs on the server racks — green and amber, blinking in patterns that look almost like code.
The servers are arranged in concentric circles.
I stop walking.

The circles are precise. Seven rings, each one slightly smaller than the last, descending toward a central column that pulses with soft blue light. It's Dante's architecture made physical. Inferno as infrastructure. The circles of Hell rendered in server racks and fiber optic cables.

The room hums. Not with electricity — I know what server rooms sound like, and this isn't it. This hum has harmonics. Overtones. It sounds like a choir singing in a frequency just below the threshold of words.
"Impressive, isn't it?" David says. "Our data processing capacity is four hundred percent above industry standard."
"I can see that." I pull out my phone, activate the audit app. "I'll need to run a systems check. Standard protocol."
"Of course. Take your time."
Leo's voice comes through my earpiece, pitched low. "I'm in. The audit mode is giving me access to the local network. Downloading now."
I walk the perimeter of the outermost circle. The servers pulse as I pass. I keep my phone raised, screen facing the racks, letting the app capture everything.

"I'm getting the emotional extraction protocols," Leo whispers. His voice is tight with controlled excitement. "The full specs. Chloe — this is the source code. The actual extraction algorithms. This is everything we need for the case."
"Good," I murmur, loud enough for the mic to catch, quiet enough that David won't hear.

"Also getting organizational charts. Internal memos. There's a quarterly report on 'deduction yields' — they literally track emotional output by volume. Trust and empathy are the highest-yield categories. Love is category three — turns out it's harder to extract at scale."
"Copy that."
I complete the circuit. The central column pulses again, and this time the hum changes pitch. The servers are adapting. Adjusting. They know I'm here.
"I need to see the archive room," I say.
David's professional smile flickers. Just for a second. A micro-lapse in the performance, like a frame drop in a video stream.
"The archive room isn't on the standard tour."

I pull up the audit clause on my phone. Section 14.3, Subsection B: "The auditing party shall be granted access to all data facilities, including but not limited to servers, storage arrays, backup systems, and archival storage."
"All data facilities," I say. "That's what the contract says. The archive room is a data facility. I have the right."

David hesitates. The hollow space behind his eyes processes the request — I can almost see it running, weighing compliance against caution, calculating what he's authorized to show and what he's been told to hide.
"The archives are in the sub-basement," he says finally. "It's... a different environment down there."
"I'm aware."
"Follow me."
The elevator is different from the express car that brought me to Floor 67. Smaller. Older. No buttons inside — David uses the keycard again, and the elevator descends.
Past the lobby. Past the ground floor. Past the building's lowest registered level. The elevator keeps going down.
The taste of the air changes. The recycled sterility of the upper floors gives way to something older. Warmer. I taste mineral and dust and a faint metallic edge that coats my tongue.

The elevator stops. The doors don't open immediately — David has to swipe his card a third time and enter a six-digit code on a keypad I can't see. Something clicks. Something heavy, mechanical, like a vault.
The doors open.

The air hits me like a physical force. Hot. Humid. Thick enough that I can feel it pressing against my skin, replacing the server room's cold with a damp heat that sinks into my clothes and clings. And the smell — soldering iron and old libraries. Flux and parchment. The scent of circuitry being born and the scent of knowledge being preserved, layered on top of each other like geological strata.
"What is this?" I ask, though I already know.
"The Archive," David says. "Everything we've ever preserved."
I step out of the elevator and into a cathedral.

That's the only word for it. The space is built like a church — a nave stretching before me, columns rising to vaulted ceilings that disappear into darkness overhead. The proportions are sacred. The scale is devotional. Every line of the architecture draws the eye forward and up, the way churches are designed to make you look toward God.
But there's no God here.
Instead of stained glass, there are screens.
Thousands of screens. Set into the walls where the windows should be. Mounted on the columns. Arranged in rows along the nave, rising in tiers toward the ceiling. Each screen displays a face.

Human faces. Eyes open. Expressions frozen in something between neutrality and absence. Not dead — archived. Preserved at the exact moment of hollowing, when the emotional core was extracted and whatever remained was filed and stored.
I walk through the nave.

My footsteps echo. The cathedral is silent except for the hum — the same hum from the server room, but amplified, multiplied, layered. 847,000 screens, each one drawing power, each one generating a frequency just below audibility. The combined sound vibrates in my chest. I can feel it in my teeth.

I find Marcus Webb's screen in the second ring. The art professor. The one who used to cry at Rothko paintings. His face is here, flat and luminous, eyes open on nothing. The screen's status readout glows beneath his chin: ARCHIVED. DEDUCTION COMPLETE.
I find Dara Okonkwo's screen two rows over. Same expression. Same emptiness. Same green status light.

I keep walking. The concentric circles of screens mirror the digital architecture I saw in the Hollowed Files — the same organizational logic made physical. The inner rings are the oldest. The outer rings are the newest. The system grows outward, like tree rings, like scar tissue.

The faces blur together. Not because they look the same — they don't. Every face is different. Different ages, different ethnicities, different configurations of features. But the expression is universal. That frozen, hollow, open-eyed nothing. The look of someone who had something taken from them and didn't even notice it was gone.
I reach the far end of the nave. The altar is a final bank of screens, the largest ones, arranged in a semi-circle like a choir. Above them, carved into the stone vault of the ceiling, a single word:
COMMERCE.
Not God. Not love. Not truth. Commerce.

I stare at it. The word is old. The stone is old. The carving has the depth and precision of something made by hands that had centuries to practice. This cathedral wasn't built in the twenty-first century. This cathedral was already here, waiting for the glass tower to be built on top of it.
847,000 faces. 847,000 archived souls, stored in a sub-basement beneath the tallest building in the Western Hemisphere, rendered in light and silicon and something that isn't electricity.
I turn around.
Mephisto is standing in the doorway of the Archive.

He's not in a suit this time. He's wearing something older — a long coat of dark material that seems to absorb light rather than reflect it. The cut is wrong for any decade I can name. The fabric drapes in a way that suggests it was tailored in a century that didn't have tailors. Or didn't need them.
His eyes are fully black now. No iris, no sclera, no pretense of humanity left. Two voids set in a face that's almost handsome, almost real, almost something you could look at without flinching.
Almost.
"You've seen enough," he says.
It's not a question.

I don't move. My body doesn't tense. My heart doesn't race. I can't feel fear, and right now, that's the most useful disability I've ever had. I stand in the nave of his cathedral, surrounded by the faces of the harvested, and I look at him the way I'd look at a line of code that's doing exactly what it was designed to do.
He gestures to the screens. The movement is graceful. Old. Practiced across so many centuries the gesture has worn smooth, like river stone.
"Do you understand now? This is what souls become when they're properly managed. This is efficiency. This is scale."

His voice is calm. Almost gentle. He's not performing. He's not projecting. He's explaining something to me the way you'd explain something to a visitor who just doesn't get the concept yet. The way you'd explain to someone that their resistance isn't just futile — it's quaint.

I look at the screens. 847,000 faces. 847,000 people who could be restored. The word on Evelyn's legal pad comes back to me: REPARATIONS. The files Leo is downloading right now. The extraction protocols. The deduction yields. The source code of hell, sitting on my phone like a loaded weapon.
I look back at Mephisto.
"I understand perfectly."
I walk past him.

He doesn't stop me. He doesn't move. He stands in the doorway of his cathedral with his old coat and his black eyes and lets me pass. I can feel his gaze on my back as I step into the elevator. I can feel it as the doors close.
The elevator rises. The heat fades. The smell of solder and parchment gives way to the sterile recycled air of the upper floors. I taste metal and chemicals and nothing else.
Floor 67. Floor 1. The lobby.

I walk out of 1 World Trade Center into the June sun. The light is normal. The air is normal. The world is normal, except 847,000 people are archived in a cathedral beneath the financial district and their jailer is an entity older than the concept of jailers.
I pull out my phone. I call Evelyn.
"I saw the Archive," I say.
Silence on the line. Then: "How bad?"

"It's a cathedral. Eight hundred forty-seven thousand screens. Each one's a person. He's keeping them — physically — in the sub-basement. It's not just data extraction, Evelyn. He's got them stored down there like artifacts."
Another silence. Longer this time. I can hear her pen moving.
"Add a seventh count," I say. "Conversion. He's physically detaining souls in a data center. That's not exploitation — that's kidnapping."
The pen stops. "Kidnapping requires a body."
"He's got their faces on screens. He's got their emotional cores in his servers. He's got them archived in a room he carved out of bedrock. If that's not detention, I don't know what is."
Evelyn takes a breath. I hear it through the phone — the sound of a lawyer deciding whether to be careful or to be right.
"Send me everything Leo downloaded," she says. "I'll draft the amendment tonight."
I hang up. I stand on the plaza of 1 World Trade Center with the sun on my face and the hollow space in my chest where my fear should be.
He let me see it.

That's the part I can't stop turning over. He didn't have to let me into the Archive. He didn't have to appear at the door in his real skin. He could have denied the audit, stalled the inspection, redirected me to a decoy. He's been doing this longer than anyone's been doing anything. He knows how to hide.
He chose to show me.

Which means he's either so confident that he doesn't care what I saw — or he wanted me to see it. Wanted me to know the scale. Wanted me to understand, in that intimate and terrible way, exactly what I'm up against.
A flex from the inside. A warning dressed up as a revelation.
I understand perfectly.
The question is whether understanding is enough, or whether I just walked into something I can't walk out of.
I put my phone away. I start walking.
The sun is warm. The city is loud. Somewhere below me, 847,000 faces glow in the dark.
